<strong>Operational status of ionocast:<: Operational status of ionocast: free, personal / hobby amateur-radio dashboard. No ads. No paywalls. No paid tiers. No commercial use. The footer carries a Ko-fi link for voluntary donations toward hosting costs: nothing is gated behind a donation, no rewards are offered, and every panel remains free for every visitor regardless of whether they donate. Five of the sources below forbid commercial use; any future ads, paywall, or paid tier would break all five simultaneously. See Commercial-use ban at the bottom.
<strong>Personal / non-commercial:</stro: Personal / non-commercial: yes. Commercial use: no (NC flow-through from GIRO).
<strong>Personal / scientific use:</stro: Personal / scientific use: yes. Commercial use: explicitly not allowed for geomagnetic indices.
<strong>Personal use:</strong> yes. <str: Personal use: yes. Commercial use: yes (the parameters are observational facts; calendar entries are not copyrightable on their own).
<strong>Quicklook caveat:</strong> the <: Quicklook caveat: the dst*.for.request feed is quicklook, not final. WDC asks that this be surfaced in the UI.
<strong>Rate limits:</strong> <strong>ha: Rate limits: hard cap of 20 requests / minute (since Oct 2023). Burst briefly tolerated. GET only; only ?query= accepted. Headers filtered, POST blocked.
<strong>Rate limits:</strong> no publish: Rate limits: no published hard cap; NCEP monitors for abuse. We refresh 4× daily on the GFS cycle (00 / 06 / 12 / 18 z + ~5 h to clear publish lag) via a GitHub Actions workflow that downloads a NOMADS subset URL (~21 MB filtered to the variables and levels we need, vs ~500 MB unfiltered).
Rate limits: no published hard limit. NWS monitors for abuse and can block IPs. Guidance: poll no faster than the product's refresh cadence (e.g. 60 s for 1-min products), back off 60 s on errors.
<strong>Rate limits:</strong> none state: Rate limits: none stated; small static CSV.
<strong>Rate limits:</strong> not number: Rate limits: not numbered; be polite, cache for hours.
<strong>Rate limits:</strong> not publis: Rate limits: not published; 5 min edge cache is considerate.
Rate limits: not published. Contact: ldoolman@uwyo.edu. They have historically asked heavy scrapers to throttle.
Rate limits: not published. Keep polling conservative; DONKI is a lightly-resourced research service.
Rate limits: not published. Official Web Service API is available.
<strong>ShareAlike:</strong> any derivat: ShareAlike: any derivative data that ionocast redistributes (beyond simple re-display) must itself be licensed CC-BY-NC-SA 4.0.
<strong>Tobias Wellnitz (DH7TW)</strong>: Tobias Wellnitz (DH7TW) and the broader WSPR community for the wspr.live infrastructure and the raw spot archive.
<strong>Trademarks:</strong> NOAA / NCEP: Trademarks: NOAA / NCEP names and logos are trademark-protected. Do not imply official endorsement.
<strong>Trademarks:</strong> NOAA / NWS: Trademarks: NOAA / NWS names and logos are trademark-protected. Do not imply official endorsement.
1-min auroral electrojet index from WDC: 1-min auroral electrojet index from WDC Kyoto (AE = AU − AL), derived from 12 northern-hemisphere magnetometers, in nT. Tracks substorm activity directly; jumps above 1000 nT during substorms.
1-minute resolution equivalent of Dst fr: 1-minute resolution equivalent of Dst from WDC Kyoto, in nT. Tracks ring-current intensity in real time. Negative values = storm main phase; below −50 nT = G1 storm, below −100 nT = G2.
15-minute standard deviation of dTEC/dt,: 15-minute standard deviation of dTEC/dt, in TECU/min. Above ~1 indicates ionospheric scintillation that fades HF, GNSS, and trans-ionospheric signals.
4-char (~town) or 6-char (~10 km). Used: 4-char (~town) or 6-char (~10 km). Used for path computations.
A surface-based or elevated atmospheric: A surface-based or elevated atmospheric duct exists in the lowest 1 km: a sufficiently strong temperature inversion or moisture lapse produces a layer where dM/dz < 0, trapping VHF/UHF signals into a horizontal waveguide. Operationally: 6 m and 2 m stations sharing the duct can work each other over hundreds to thousands of km independent of ionospheric propagation.
Achieved SNR minus required SNR, in dB,: Achieved SNR minus required SNR, in dB, for the configured station on the best-margin reference path. Positive = comfortable, zero = at the decoder threshold, negative = below threshold. The tier converts this into a reliability bucket via the per-band sigma; the same margin can yield different tiers depending on path uncertainty.
Alert tier. A threshold has been crossed: Alert tier. A threshold has been crossed and HF impact is active right now: M5+/X1+ flares (R2/R3), Kp 6-7 (G2/G3), Dst ≤ −100 nT, S2/S3 proton events, strong HSS, geomagnetic storm main phase, D-RAP ≥ 10 MHz at QTH. Expect active HF degradation on affected paths.
Amateur radio band by wavelength: 160 m: Amateur radio band by wavelength: 160 m at 1.8 MHz down to 2 m at 144 MHz. Each band has its own propagation character: low bands (160-40 m) for night and short-skip, mid bands (30-20 m) for steady DX, upper bands (17-10 m) for solar-cycle DX, VHF (6 m / 2 m) for line-of-sight + Es / aurora / meteor scatter. The SNR budget for each band is computed at the band's ITU-R P.533 reference frequency (1.85 / 3.65 / 5.36 / 7.10 / 10.10 / 14.10 / 18.10 / 21.10 / 24.90 / 28.20 MHz on 160-10 m; 50 / 144 MHz on VHF). Within-band variation in predicted margin is small (< 0.5 dB free-space-loss spread across a band's width) so the reference frequency is a representative anchor rather than the operator's exact dial setting.
Amateur radio operators planning an oper: Amateur radio operators planning an operating session need to answer a deceptively simple question: which bands are open right now, and how reliably for the station they are actually using? Existing tools serve fragments of the problem. VOACAP delivers rigorous P.533 predictions but runs as a batch tool with no real-time inputs. Solar-condition tables key on Kp and SFI without path geometry. MUF maps draw observed contours without an SNR context. ionocast was built to occupy the intersection: real-time, physics-grounded, station-aware, and privacy-preserving.
Angular half-width of a coronal mass eje: Angular half-width of a coronal mass ejection as fit from coronagraph imagery. A 30° halfAngle CME spans 60° of sky and is more likely to be Earth-directed; very narrow CMEs (< 15°) typically miss Earth. Reported by CCMC's WSA-ENLIL+Cone fit on each DONKI entry.
Atmospheric refractivity N at the lowest: Atmospheric refractivity N at the lowest sounding sample, computed from temperature, pressure, and water-vapor pressure: N = 77.6 / T_K · (P_mbar + 4810 · e_mbar / T_K). Typical sea-level values are 280-320 N-units; the absolute level matters less than how fast it falls with height (dN/dh).
Aurora HP {v} GW. 6 m aurora-E possible: Aurora HP {v} GW. 6 m aurora-E possible at high latitudes.
Aurora HP {v} GW. VHF aurora propagation: Aurora HP {v} GW. VHF aurora propagation likely.
Band frequency divided by the modeled pa: Band frequency divided by the modeled path MUF. < 0.85 stable (FOT region); 0.85-1.0 marginal/near-MUF; > 1.0 above MUF, meaning no F2 support on this path. ITU-R P.533 defines FOT = 0.85 × MUF.
Bouncing signals off the Moon for ultra-: Bouncing signals off the Moon for ultra-DX VHF/UHF contacts. Path loss is ~250 dB total; needs high power, large antennas, and digital modes (Q65, JT65). Always available when both stations see the Moon, independent of ionosphere.
Bright arcs are filaments/prominences. E: Bright arcs are filaments/prominences. Erupting ones can launch CMEs toward Earth.
Built by Toprak Kilic, TA1BUT. Contact:: Built by Toprak Kilic, TA1BUT. Contact: ta1but@toprakkilic.com
CCMC has not yet run a magnetospheric-im: CCMC has not yet run a magnetospheric-impact simulation for this CME. Either the event is too recent (impact studies typically post within hours of detection) or the CME is judged not Earth-directed enough to warrant the run. Without an impact study, no Kp peak or arrival time prediction is available.
Classification of the current geomagneti: Classification of the current geomagnetic disturbance source: CME (transient coronal mass ejection shock) or HSS (high-speed solar wind stream from a coronal hole). CME-driven storms typically have a faster recovery (~8 h decay timescale); HSS-driven storms decay more slowly (~24 h) because the stream keeps arriving for days. Determines how long the storm tail lingers in the propagation budget.
Cloudflare Pages serves the static files: Cloudflare Pages serves the static files and proxies the upstream API requests through edge functions. Cloudflare's privacy and logging policies apply to that layer; ionocast does not add its own logging on top.
Could not reach SWPC alerts API.
Could not reach SWPC Kp API.
Could not reach SWPC solar wind API.
Courtesy of NASA/SDO and the AIA, EVE, a: Courtesy of NASA/SDO and the AIA, EVE, and HMI science teams.
Critical frequency of sporadic-E patches: Critical frequency of sporadic-E patches (thin, dense, transient ionization in the E region), in MHz. > 5 MHz can support 10 m hops; > 8 MHz supports 6 m Es; > 25 MHz needed for 2 m Es.

Critical frequency of sporadic-E patches at the nearest digisonde, in MHz; same quantity as foEs in the ionosphere panel. > 5 MHz can support 10 m hops, > 8 MHz supports 6 m Es, > 25 MHz needed for 2 m Es. Sporadic-E is short-lived (minutes to hours) and short-skip (typical single hop ~2000 km).
Critical frequency of the regular E laye: Critical frequency of the regular E layer (~110 km altitude), in MHz. Daytime only; rises and falls smoothly with the solar zenith angle. Sets the floor for E-layer absorption on lower HF.
Cross-equatorial chordal propagation off: Cross-equatorial chordal propagation off F-region irregularities. Active on 14-60 MHz when both endpoints sit on opposite sides of the magnetic dip equator with |dipLat| ≥ 10°, and the path midpoint local time is in 17:00-23:00 (afternoon to evening). Bonus magnitude scales with solar cycle: peak openings reach 15+ dB recovery, moderate cycle is 8-12 dB. Sporadic in nature, works for 1-3 hour evening windows, not all day.
Current GOES XRS-B (0.1-0.8 nm) X-ray fl: Current GOES XRS-B (0.1-0.8 nm) X-ray flux, classified by decade: A=10⁻⁸, B=10⁻⁷, C=10⁻⁶, M=10⁻⁵, X=10⁻⁴ W/m². M and X class flares cause HF blackouts on the sunlit side within minutes (NOAA R-scale: M1→R1, M5→R2, X1→R3, X10→R4, X20→R5).
Currently active meteor showers from the: Currently active meteor showers from the IMO calendar; relevant for 6 m/2 m meteor scatter.
D-RAP {v} MHz at QTH. Local HF blackout.
D-RAP {v} MHz at QTH. Lower HF degraded.
D-region attenuation drops sharply at th: D-region attenuation drops sharply at the terminator (sunrise / sunset line); low-band paths that include the terminator at the path midpoint see a brief absorption-window opening. Active on bands ≤ 14 MHz when the path midpoint is within ~6° of zenith-grazing solar angle. Lasts 30-60 minutes per terminator crossing. Most useful for 80m and 40m DX morning / evening windows.
Daily geomagnetic activity index, comput: Daily geomagnetic activity index, computed from the eight 3-hour Kp values of the day converted to a linear-scale ap and averaged. Range: 0-7 quiet, 8-15 unsettled, 16-29 active, 30+ storm. Used in the 27-day outlook as the slow-tracker analogue of Kp.
Daily provisional International Sunspot: Daily provisional International Sunspot Number (EISN) from the SILSO World Data Center in Brussels. Counts visible sunspots weighted by group complexity. Tracks closely with F10.7 and conditions on the upper HF bands (10-15 m).
Dark patches are coronal holes - fast so: Dark patches are coronal holes - fast solar wind that disturbs HF in 2-3 days.
Dark spots = sunspots = active regions. More spots generally mean better HF.
Default for {type}: height {h} m, gain {: Default for {type}: height {h} m, gain {g} dBi
Development is AI-assisted: the codebase: Development is AI-assisted: the codebase and calibration sweeps are produced in extensive collaboration with large language models. All physics, data sources, and validation methodology are operator-reviewed; AI accelerates implementation without replacing the domain judgment that anchors the model's calibration and limitations.
Direct ionospheric measurements: nearest: Direct ionospheric measurements: nearest GIRO digisonde (foF2/foEs/hmF2), GNSS-derived total electron content, and 30 MHz cosmic-noise absorption.
Donations are unsolicited beyond the lin: Donations are unsolicited beyond the link itself; no rewards, no perks, no tiers.
Dst {v} nT. Ring-current depressed; HF u: Dst {v} nT. Ring-current depressed; HF unsettled.
Dst {v} nT. Storm main phase; F-region d: Dst {v} nT. Storm main phase; F-region depressed.
Dst: WDC Kyoto, quicklook / provisional,: Dst: WDC Kyoto, quicklook / provisional, non-commercial.
Each upstream data series is attributed: Each upstream data series is attributed below under its own license. See the
Earth-directed coronal mass ejections fr: Earth-directed coronal mass ejections from the NASA DONKI catalog with predicted arrival times.
Electron {e} MeV integral flux exceeded: Electron {e} MeV integral flux exceeded {p} pfu.
Estimated meteor scatter rate, meteors p: Estimated meteor scatter rate, meteors per hour. Background ~10/h from sporadic meteors; major showers (Perseids, Geminids, Leonids) reach 100+/h. Productive on 6 m and 2 m via short-burst forward scatter off ionization trails.
Every data source, image, and embedded w: Every data source, image, and embedded widget used by ionocast, with its license, required attribution string, and any constraints that affect how the site can be operated.
Every output is the deterministic result: Every output is the deterministic result of explicit physical equations applied to the published data feeds. There are no machine-learning components anywhere in the prediction path. Validation is honest about its scope: the offline harness is intended for regression detection during model development, not as a production-grade reliability claim. ionocast is free, non-commercial, and runs without advertising or third-party telemetry.
Extreme tier. The most severe end of the: Extreme tier. The most severe end of the NOAA scales: R4-R5 X-ray flares (X10+), G4-G5 geomagnetic storms (Kp 8-9), S4-S5 radiation storms (≥ 10 000 pfu), or Dst ≤ −250 nT ring-current intensification. Operationally: widespread HF blackouts persisting hours-to-days, transpolar paths closed, low bands degraded, auroral oval expanded to mid-latitudes.
F-region irregularity scatter brings sig: F-region irregularity scatter brings signals through above the standard MUF. Fires when the band is above the climatological MUF AND foF2 varies meaningfully across hops (heterogeneous path). Intensity scales with above-MUF excess and foF2 spread; saturates at ~15 dB recovery. Less reliable than F2; gives a partial recovery rather than full.
F2 critical frequency at the midpoint of: F2 critical frequency at the midpoint of this path, in MHz, sampled from the kc2g grid. Drives the modeled MUF for the path.
Five-level operator-facing verdict for t: Five-level operator-facing verdict for this band: excellent, good, fair, poor, or closed. The bucket is an ITU-R P.842 circuit-reliability bucket: excellent ≥ 95 %, good ≥ 80 %, fair ≥ 50 %, poor ≥ 20 %, closed < 20 %, where reliability is the probability the achieved SNR clears the mode's required SNR for the configured station.
foF2 critical frequency, real-time ionos: foF2 critical frequency, real-time ionospheric density (kc2g)
Forecast confidence: ±{n} dB during dist: Forecast confidence: ±{n} dB during disturbed window.
Forecast σ +{n} dB. Disturbed Kp slot in: Forecast σ +{n} dB. Disturbed Kp slot in the next 6 to 12 h widens tier uncertainty; treat upper-band verdicts cautiously.
Forecast σ +{n} dB. Mildly disturbed win: Forecast σ +{n} dB. Mildly disturbed window ahead; tier verdicts on upper bands soften.
Forward scatter off the ionization trail: Forward scatter off the ionization trails left by meteors entering the upper atmosphere (~85-105 km). Bursts last fractions of a second to several seconds; sustained QSOs require digital modes (MSK144, FSK441). Productive on 6 m and 2 m, especially during major showers.
Geomagnetic storm main phase{head}. F-re: Geomagnetic storm main phase{head}. F-region MUF depressed; high latitudes most affected.
Geomagnetic storm phase classified from: Geomagnetic storm phase classified from Dst + Kp + storm-lag kernel: initial (sudden compression, Dst positive), main (Dst dropping rapidly toward minimum), recovery (Dst returning to zero while kpEffective trails kpNow), or active (between thresholds). HF behaviour over the next several hours follows directly: main-phase HF degrades fast on polar paths, recovery-phase upper HF lags.
GFZ Potsdam high-cadence successor to Kp: GFZ Potsdam high-cadence successor to Kp, published every 30 minutes since 2022. Same 0-9 scale and physics as Kp, but catches sub-storms that the slower 3-hour Kp averages out.
GloTEC, global total electron content (N: GloTEC, global total electron content (NOAA SWPC)
GNSS amplitude scintillation index, dime: GNSS amplitude scintillation index, dimensionless 0-1. Above 0.5 = significant scintillation, i.e. rapid signal fading on satellite and trans-ionospheric links.
GOES integral flux of energetic electron: GOES integral flux of energetic electrons above 2 MeV at GEO, in particles cm⁻² s⁻¹ sr⁻¹. Sustained values above 1000 indicate internal-charging risk for satellites in geostationary orbit.
GOES integral flux of protons with E > 1: GOES integral flux of protons with E > 1 MeV, in pfu (proton flux units, particles cm⁻² s⁻¹ sr⁻¹). The lowest-energy GOES proton channel. Useful as a solar energetic particle (SEP) onset detector: ≥1 MeV typically rises ~1 hour before the canonical ≥10 MeV channel crosses the S1 threshold, giving early warning of polar-cap absorption events.

GOES integral flux of protons with E > 10 MeV, in pfu (proton flux units, particles cm⁻² s⁻¹ sr⁻¹). NOAA radiation-storm S-scale: S1 at 10 pfu, S2 at 100, S3 at 1000, S4 at 10⁴, S5 at 10⁵. Drives polar-cap absorption (PCA) on HF paths crossing the polar regions.

GOES integral flux of protons with E > 100 MeV, in pfu. Hard SEPs that penetrate deep into the D-region (down to ~30 km altitude) and produce the most severe transpolar HF blackouts. Quiet-sun background is well below 1 pfu; even 1 pfu is operationally significant. Also a radiation-dose driver for high-altitude flight crews.
Hale magnetic classification for a sunsp: Hale magnetic classification for a sunspot group with a single dominant magnetic polarity. Smallest and quietest configuration; near-zero flare probability.
Hale magnetic classification for a sunspot group with two distinct opposite-polarity regions cleanly separated. Common configuration; modest flare risk (typically C-class, occasional M).

Hale magnetic classification for a sunspot group with two opposite polarities but irregular intermixing along the polarity inversion line. Stronger flare risk; M-class flares likely, X-class possible.
Hale magnetic classification for the mos: Hale magnetic classification for the most complex configuration: opposite polarities sharing a single penumbra (delta), intermixed (gamma), within a generally bipolar region (beta). High X-class flare probability; the regions that produce the most extreme flares typically carry this classification.
Higher TEC = denser ionosphere = higher: Higher TEC = denser ionosphere = higher MUF. Low TEC regions have poor HF propagation.
Highest frequency reflected at vertical: Highest frequency reflected at vertical incidence from the F2 layer over the digisonde, in MHz. The hard ceiling for NVIS. Multiply by M(3000)F2 (~3) to estimate the 3000 km path MUF.
How likely the Tier label is to stay put: How likely the Tier label is to stay put if the true margin moves to its expected value. Computed as Φ(σ-distance to the nearest tier boundary). 50% means the prediction sits right on a tier boundary and a small shift in conditions would flip the verdict; 84% means 1σ inside the bucket (the verdict has comfortable margin from either neighbouring tier); ≥97% means 2σ inside (essentially locked-in). Bucket-width-independent: a centred Fair verdict reads the same as a centred Excellent verdict if both sit equally far from their nearest boundary. A high Stability does not mean the band is open, that's what Tier says, it means the Tier label itself is unlikely to change.
If any individual upstream operator read: If any individual upstream operator reads "non-commercial" or "personal hobby use" more strictly than this and objects to the donate link, the response is to drop the affected panel, not the donate link. The audit trail of which upstream operators have been contacted and what they replied is kept in docs/UPSTREAM-CONTACTS.md in the project repository.
If ionocast ever needs to become commerc: If ionocast ever needs to become commercial, every one of these sources needs an alternate license or removal from the site.
If your name or organization should be o: If your name or organization should be on this page and is missing, please open an issue on the repository or contact the author at the email below.
IMF Bz {v} nT. Substorm likely within 1: IMF Bz {v} nT. Substorm likely within 1 to 3 h.
Implementation and calibration sweeps we: Implementation and calibration sweeps were AI-assisted: large language models accelerated the code-side work and the validation harness loops. All physics, data-source choices, calibration anchors, and operational decisions are operator-reviewed and operator-approved; the AI accelerates implementation without replacing the domain judgment that anchors the model's calibration and known limitations. The whitepaper is the canonical record of those judgments.
Informational alert. Either a SWPC summa: Informational alert. Either a SWPC summary / forecast / advisory bulletin (event recap, scheduled outlook, operational note) or a soft alert flagging a context-relevant condition (storm recovery still loading the F-region, mild forecast σ inflation). Color is gray; no immediate operational impact.
ionocast itself is licensed under the <s: ionocast itself is licensed under the GNU General Public License, version 3 or any later version (GPL-3.0-or-later). See the LICENSE file in the repository for the full text.
ionocast keeps operator data on the devi: ionocast keeps operator data on the device wherever possible. The QTH is one exception: two panels send it to ionocast's own proxies for nearest-station lookups, detailed below. Beyond that, no location, settings, or telemetry leaves the device. Specifically:
ionocast must stay free and non-commerci: ionocast must stay free and non-commercial. Adding ads, a paywall, or a paid tier breaks all of these licenses at once:
ionocast needs JavaScript. Most panels a: ionocast needs JavaScript. Most panels are derived live in the browser from SWPC, kc2g, GIRO, and other live sources.
ionocast would not exist without the ope: ionocast would not exist without the open-data infrastructure built and maintained by the people behind every source listed at the top of this page. Sincere thanks to:
Issues, pull requests, or questions can: Issues, pull requests, or questions can be opened on the GitHub repository.
ITU-R P.453 / NTIA classification of the: ITU-R P.453 / NTIA classification of the lowest-1-km gradient: standard (no enhancement), super-refractive (extended VHF range), or ducting (waveguide traps signal over 100s to 1000s of km). Values below the column come from this row's dN/dh.
Ko-fi proceeds offset hosting and domain: Ko-fi proceeds offset hosting and domain costs; ionocast is not a business and has no revenue model.
Larger oval = HF disruption on polar pat: Larger oval = HF disruption on polar paths; VHF aurora propagation possible at K≥5.
Location and station profile. The SNR bu: Location and station profile. The SNR budget uses these values to compute per-band verdicts tailored to your setup.
Maximum frequency reflecting straight up: Maximum frequency reflecting straight up. NVIS operators: use frequencies below this value. MUF per-path is in the table above.
Maximum usable frequency for a 3000 km h: Maximum usable frequency for a 3000 km hop along this path, in MHz, sampled from the kc2g IRI-assimilated MUF grid. Above this frequency, signals pass into space rather than refract back. Modeled, not measured; see MUF(3000)F2 for the nearest digisonde value.
Maximum usable frequency for a 3000 km hop, computed directly from the local digisonde ionogram as foF2 × M(3000)F2. Ground truth for the nearest path; produced by ARTIST-5 autoscaling.
Median signal-to-noise ratio of WSPR spo: Median signal-to-noise ratio of WSPR spots received on this band over the last hour, in dB on a 2.5 kHz noise reference. Higher = the band is delivering stronger signals right now.
Meteor shower is on the leading edge of: Meteor shower is on the leading edge of its activity envelope per the IMO calendar (a few days before the active window). Floor lift fires in this phase too because the catalogued ZHR rises smoothly into peak.
Meteor shower is on the trailing edge of: Meteor shower is on the trailing edge of its activity envelope per the IMO calendar (a few days past peak). The ZHR is dropping back toward background; ionocast does not apply the floor lift in this phase.
Meteor shower is within ±2 days of the I: Meteor shower is within ±2 days of the IMO catalog peak. ionocast applies the meteor-scatter floor lift on 6 m / 2 m during this phase.
MS only (meteor scatter as last resort): MS only (meteor scatter as last resort)
MUF on a radial basket of paths sampled: MUF on a radial basket of paths sampled relative to QTH: 12 bearings × 6 distance rings (2.5 / 4 / 6 / 9 / 12 / 16 Mm), 72 paths total. The shortest ring sits at the geometric minimum for a single F2 hop; below that propagation is NVIS-mode (handled separately) rather than F2-hop. The longest-viable ring per bearing is shown here for readability; the band-table's per-band best-path selector consumes the full basket. Midpoint MUF from the kc2g IRI-assimilated grid. f/MUF ≤ 0.85 = stable (FOT), ≤ 1.0 = marginal, > 1.0 = no F2 support.
No content is gated; every panel is free: No content is gated; every panel is free to every visitor whether or not they donate.
No cookies. No analytics. No third-party: No cookies. No analytics. No third-party tracking pixels. No advertising networks. The page does not register a service worker.
no Es or aurora; tropospheric / meteor s: no Es or aurora; tropospheric / meteor scatter only
No kc2g TEC station near QTH.
NOAA G-scale, extreme tier (Kp = 9). HF: NOAA G-scale, extreme tier (Kp = 9). HF radio may be impossible in many areas for 1 to 2 days; aurora visible as far as equatorial latitudes.
NOAA G-scale, minor tier (Kp = 5). Weak: NOAA G-scale, minor tier (Kp = 5). Weak power-grid fluctuations at high latitudes; HF fade on polar paths; aurora visible at high latitudes.
NOAA G-scale, moderate tier (Kp = 6). HF: NOAA G-scale, moderate tier (Kp = 6). HF propagation can fade at higher latitudes; aurora visible to mid-latitudes.
NOAA G-scale, severe tier (Kp = 8). Spor: NOAA G-scale, severe tier (Kp = 8). Sporadic HF radio propagation; extensive surface charging; aurora visible to low latitudes.
NOAA G-scale, strong tier (Kp = 7). Inte: NOAA G-scale, strong tier (Kp = 7). Intermittent HF radio problems; surface charging on satellites; aurora visible to lower mid-latitudes.
NOAA R-scale, extreme tier (X20+ X-ray f: NOAA R-scale, extreme tier (X20+ X-ray flare). Complete HF blackout on entire sunlit side for hours; navigation outages 1+ day. Less than 1 event per solar cycle on average.
NOAA R-scale, minor tier (M1-M4.9 X-ray: NOAA R-scale, minor tier (M1-M4.9 X-ray flare). Sunlit-side HF degraded for ~tens of minutes; navigation and satellite data flow can briefly drop. Several events per month at solar maximum.
NOAA R-scale, moderate tier (M5-M9.9 X-r: NOAA R-scale, moderate tier (M5-M9.9 X-ray flare). Wider-area HF blackouts on sunlit side; ~1 h recovery typical. Tens of events per year at solar maximum.
NOAA R-scale, severe tier (X10-X19.9 X-r: NOAA R-scale, severe tier (X10-X19.9 X-ray flare). HF radio blackout on most of the sunlit side for 1 to 2 h; navigation widely degraded. Rare, about 8 events per 11-year solar cycle.
NOAA R-scale, strong tier (X1-X9.9 X-ray: NOAA R-scale, strong tier (X1-X9.9 X-ray flare). Wide-area HF blackout on sunlit side for ~1 h; satellite navigation impaired for hours. ~10 events per year at solar maximum.
NOAA S-scale, extreme tier (≥ 100 000 pf: NOAA S-scale, extreme tier (≥ 100 000 pfu). Extreme polar-cap absorption; transpolar HF blacked out for the duration of the event. Less than 1 event per solar cycle on average.
NOAA S-scale, minor tier (≥ 10 MeV proto: NOAA S-scale, minor tier (≥ 10 MeV proton flux ≥ 10 pfu). Polar-cap absorption building; HF on transpolar paths degrades. ~50 events per solar cycle.
NOAA S-scale, moderate tier (≥ 100 pfu). Polar-cap absorption active; transpolar HF paths typically closed. ~25 events per solar cycle.
NOAA S-scale, severe tier (≥ 10 000 pfu): NOAA S-scale, severe tier (≥ 10 000 pfu). Severe polar-cap absorption; transpolar HF blacked out for days; satellite operations impacted. ~3 events per solar cycle.
NOAA S-scale, strong tier (≥ 1 000 pfu). Strong polar-cap absorption; transpolar HF blacked out for hours-to-days. ~10 events per solar cycle.
NOAA-assigned 4-digit number for a sunsp: NOAA-assigned 4-digit number for a sunspot group. Numbering is monotonic across cycles (does not reset). Used by SWPC to track an individual region's flare history and report its M-class / X-class probabilities.
North-south component of the interplanet: North-south component of the interplanetary magnetic field at L1 (~1 h upstream of Earth), in nT. Sustained Bz < −5 nT is the empirical storm trigger: the magnetosphere opens, energy couples in, geomagnetic storms begin. Best near-real-time storm predictor we have.
Number of meteors a single observer woul: Number of meteors a single observer would see per hour at peak shower activity, with the radiant directly overhead and a clear sky to magnitude 6.5. ionocast uses ZHR ≥ 20 as the threshold for upgrading the VHF meteor-scatter floor on 6 m / 2 m during the predawn window.
Number of WSPR spots received on this ba: Number of WSPR spots received on this band in the last 60 minutes. A direct activity proxy: more spots = more stations getting through to receivers worldwide. Doesn't measure SNR, just whether the band is alive.
Obliquity factor from the digisonde iono: Obliquity factor from the digisonde ionogram for a 3000 km hop. Multiply foF2 by M(3000)F2 to get MUF(3000)F2. Typically 2.8-3.3 depending on hmF2.
Of the 72 paths sampled around your QTH: Of the 72 paths sampled around your QTH (12 compass bearings × 6 distance rings: 2500 / 4000 / 6000 / 9000 / 12000 / 16000 km), the one with the highest predicted SNR margin on this band, labelled by compass direction and distance. The distance is shown in megametres (1 Mm = 1000 km, so 9.0 Mm = 9000 km), more compact than "9000 km" in a narrow column. Tells you which way to point and how far you can usefully reach: the band is most viable in that direction right now. Sub-2500 km contacts (NVIS) are handled separately, not in this basket.
On 2 m and above when no ionospheric mod: On 2 m and above when no ionospheric mode is open, EME (Moon bounce) remains the only true skywave option. Tropospheric ducting may also extend line-of-sight contacts. Both require effort and equipment well above casual.
On a VHF band where Es and aurora are bo: On a VHF band where Es and aurora are both absent, meteor scatter is the only ionospheric mode left. Bursty, requires digital, but works year-round (background ~10 meteors/h, much higher during showers). Tropospheric ducting may also help line-of-sight extension.
Operator settings (transmit power, anten: Operator settings (transmit power, antenna type, antenna gain, antenna height, mode, noise environment) live in the same localStorage and never leave the device.
Ordinary HF skywave propagation: refract: Ordinary HF skywave propagation: refraction off the F2 layer (~300 km altitude). The dominant DX mode on 30-10 m. Limited by the maximum usable frequency (MUF), which depends on solar activity, time of day, and path geometry.
OVATION Prime-derived auroral hemispheri: OVATION Prime-derived auroral hemispheric power proxy, in GW. ≥ 50 GW = aurora-E propagation possible on VHF (signals scatter off the auroral E region; characteristic raspy CW note).
Peak height of the F2 layer over the dig: Peak height of the F2 layer over the digisonde, in km. Sets the geometry of the hop: higher hmF2 means longer single-hop distances at the same MUF.
Per-band predicted tier, SNR margin, pro: Per-band predicted tier, SNR margin, propagation mode (Es / aurora-E / meteor scatter), and best destination from the physics model, alongside foEs from the nearest digisonde, the Es-MUF / band ratio, OVATION aurora hemispheric power, and tropospheric refractivity gradient. Above 2 m is line-of-sight / tropospheric only.

Per-band predicted tier, SNR margin, propagation mode, and best destination from the physics model, alongside live WSPR median SNR + spot counts (last 60 min), the f/MUF ratio on the best path, and modeled D-region absorption at the band frequency.
Predicted daily F10.7 (SFI) and planetar: Predicted daily F10.7 (SFI) and planetary Ap for the next 27 days from the ISES weekly outlook. Use to anticipate when conditions will favor the upper bands and when geomagnetic disturbance is expected.
Predicted Kp by 3-h period. Orange = G1: Predicted Kp by 3-h period. Orange = G1 threshold (Kp 5); red = G2 (Kp 6).
Predicted peak Kp value the CME is expec: Predicted peak Kp value the CME is expected to drive at Earth, from the CCMC WSA-ENLIL+Cone simulation attached to the DONKI entry. Quotes only what the model produced; uncertainty is wide (typically ±1 Kp) and arrival timing has ±6-8 h spread.
Preliminary NASA CCMC DONKI research dat: Preliminary NASA CCMC DONKI research data, not for operational use.
Probability of a minor-to-moderate HF ra: Probability of a minor-to-moderate HF radio blackout over the forecast window. R1 = M1 flare (minor blackout on sunlit side for tens of minutes); R2 = M5 flare (moderate, wider-area degradation).
Probability of a solar radiation storm a: Probability of a solar radiation storm at S1 or higher. Driven by solar energetic protons reaching Earth; degrades HF on polar paths via polar-cap absorption. Thresholds: S1 at 10 pfu (≥10 MeV), rising by factors of 10 to S5.
Probability of a strong-or-greater HF ra: Probability of a strong-or-greater HF radio blackout. R3 = X1 flare (wide-area HF blackout for about an hour on sunlit side); R4 = X10; R5 = X20 with days of HF disruption.
Probability of Kp ≥ 5 (minor geomagnetic: Probability of Kp ≥ 5 (minor geomagnetic storm) within the forecast window. Minor power-grid effects, HF fade on polar paths, aurora visible at high latitudes.
Probability of Kp ≥ 6 (moderate-or-stron: Probability of Kp ≥ 6 (moderate-or-stronger geomagnetic storm) within the forecast window. HF fade on higher-latitude paths; aurora visible to mid-latitudes.
Probability of Kp ≥ 7 (strong-or-stronge: Probability of Kp ≥ 7 (strong-or-stronger geomagnetic storm) within the forecast window. Intermittent HF problems; surface charging on satellites; aurora visible to lower mid-latitudes.
Proton event: {e} MeV integral flux exce: Proton event: {e} MeV integral flux exceeded {p} pfu.
Proton flux {v} pfu (S1). Polar cap abso: Proton flux {v} pfu (S1). Polar cap absorption building; polar HF degraded.
Proton flux {v} pfu (S2). Polar cap abso: Proton flux {v} pfu (S2). Polar cap absorption moderate; polar HF closed.
Proton flux {v} pfu (S3). Polar cap abso: Proton flux {v} pfu (S3). Polar cap absorption strong; transpolar paths closed.
Proton flux {v} pfu (S4). Polar cap abso: Proton flux {v} pfu (S4). Polar cap absorption severe; transpolar HF blacked out.
Proton flux {v} pfu (S5). Polar cap abso: Proton flux {v} pfu (S5). Polar cap absorption extreme; transpolar HF blacked out for days.
Quasi-log 0-9 geomagnetic disturbance in: Quasi-log 0-9 geomagnetic disturbance index, planetary average over 13 subauroral observatories, updated every 3 hours. 0-1 quiet, 2-3 unsettled, 4 active, 5+ storm. NOAA G-scale: Kp5→G1 (minor) ... Kp9→G5 (extreme). Higher Kp degrades HF, especially polar paths.
RADIO (Type II / IV solar radio sweep): RADIO (Type II / IV solar radio sweep)
Real-time planetary geomagnetic state. K: Real-time planetary geomagnetic state. Kp is the standard 3-h index; Hp30/Sym-H/AE catch substorms that Kp averages out.
Real-time solar wind at L1 (~1 hour upst: Real-time solar wind at L1 (~1 hour upstream of Earth). Southward Bz (negative) is the empirical storm trigger; sustained > 500 km/s solar-wind speed signals a coronal-hole stream.
Recent storm still settling (effective K: Recent storm still settling (effective Kp {e} vs current Kp {k}); upper HF recovery lags by hours.
Red/orange = HF signals absorbed in the: Red/orange = HF signals absorbed in the D-layer before reaching the F-layer. Avoid those paths.
Refractivity gradient across the lowest: Refractivity gradient across the lowest 1 km of the nearest radiosonde sounding, in N-units per km. The same metric the ducting table below classifies on: dN/dh > −79 is standard atmosphere, −79 to −157 super-refractive (extended VHF range), below −157 a duct forms (signals trapped in a waveguide). Both 6 m and 2 m share this cell because the gradient is per-station, not per-band.

Refractivity gradient across the lowest 1 km of the sounding, in N-units per km. dN/dh > −79 is standard atmosphere; −79 to −157 super-refractive (signals bend more strongly than line-of-sight); below −157 a duct forms (signals are trapped in a waveguide). The −157 threshold comes from modified-refractivity theory: dM/dz = dN/dz + 0.157 N/m, so a duct exists when dM/dz < 0.
Reliability < 20 %: at the configured st: Reliability < 20 %: at the configured station, almost no attempt completes a QSO. Could be over-MUF, blocked by D-region absorption, or below the noise floor. May still produce isolated WSPR spots from extreme stations.
Reliability ≥ 20 %: the band is alive bu: Reliability ≥ 20 %: the band is alive but signals are weak. FT8 and WSPR will get through, SSB intermittent. Operator can make contacts with effort and persistence.
Reliability ≥ 50 %: roughly half-and-hal: Reliability ≥ 50 %: roughly half-and-half. Ordinary contacts work but require patience; weak DX and digital modes succeed where SSB struggles. The center of the verdict scale.
Reliability ≥ 80 %: about 4 of 5 attempt: Reliability ≥ 80 %: about 4 of 5 attempts complete a QSO at the configured station. Strong, comfortable signals; ragchews and DX both viable. Threshold scales with each band's prediction sigma.
Reliability ≥ 95 %: the configured stati: Reliability ≥ 95 %: the configured station's achieved SNR clears the mode's required SNR essentially every attempt. Wall-to-wall S9+ signals; QSOs complete on the first call. Threshold scales with each band's prediction sigma.
Same model, southern hemisphere. Aurora: Same model, southern hemisphere. Aurora HP scalar is in the VHF table; CME arrivals are in the Outlook section.
Select closest match; expand advanced to: Select closest match; expand advanced to adjust height and gain.
Settings (location, theme, power, antenn: Settings (location, theme, power, antenna, mode, noise)
SFI (Solar Flux Index, equivalent to F10: SFI (Solar Flux Index, equivalent to F10.7)
Single-hop refraction off thin, dense, t: Single-hop refraction off thin, dense, transient ionization in the E layer (~110 km altitude). Active when foEs is high; supports 10-50 MHz hops over typical distances of 1000-2000 km. Onset and ending are sudden; openings last minutes to hours.
Soft alert: a deep Dst depression (≤ −50: Soft alert: a deep Dst depression (≤ −50 nT) is loading the storm-lag kernel. The F-region is in active main-phase suppression: MUF drops 5 to 30 % at midlatitudes, more at high latitudes; absorption rises; auroral oval expands. Distinct from RECOV (recovery) and from the per-Kp G-pills (severity by index value).
Soft alert: OVATION hemispheric-power pr: Soft alert: OVATION hemispheric-power proxy is elevated. ≥ 50 GW supports 6 m aurora-E at high latitudes; ≥ 100 GW opens VHF aurora propagation more broadly. Signals scatter off the auroral E region with characteristic raspy tones.
Soft alert: solar-wind speed at L1 susta: Soft alert: solar-wind speed at L1 sustained ≥ 600 km/s without sharply negative Bz signals a corotating-stream interaction (HSS / CIR), not a CME shock. HSS storms are mild-Dst but long-tail (24+ h recovery). Operator effect: 10/12 m get worse, 17/20 m unsettled, low bands often quiet down. ≥ 800 km/s escalates to alert level.
Soft alert: SWPC D-Region Absorption Pre: Soft alert: SWPC D-Region Absorption Prediction model shows the highest absorbed frequency at your QTH is elevated. ≥ 5 MHz degrades low-HF (40 m / 80 m / 160 m); ≥ 10 MHz is a local HF blackout. Caused by solar X-ray flares or proton events.
Soft alert: Sym-H (1-minute Dst equivale: Soft alert: Sym-H (1-minute Dst equivalent) shows ring-current intensification. Dst ≤ −50 nT corresponds to G1 onset, ≤ −100 nT to G2, ≤ −250 nT to G4. Severity is carried by the pill color (watch / alert / extreme); the body shows the actual Dst value.
Soft alert: the exponentially-weighted e: Soft alert: the exponentially-weighted effective Kp (peaked 2 h in the past, 8 h decay) is substantially higher than the instantaneous Kp. The geomagnetic field has relaxed but the F-region is still catching up, so upper-HF verdicts trail the published Kp by hours. Transient; clears as the kernel decays.
Soft alert: the north-south component of: Soft alert: the north-south component of the interplanetary magnetic field at L1 is strongly negative. Sustained Bz < −5 nT opens the magnetosphere to solar-wind energy and is the empirical trigger for substorms and geomagnetic storms; Bz ≤ −10 nT typically precedes a substorm by minutes.
Soft alert: the SWPC 3-day Kp forecast c: Soft alert: the SWPC 3-day Kp forecast contains disturbed slots in the next 6 to 12 h, which the model translates into widened tier-prediction uncertainty (forecast σ). Verdicts on upper bands soften: a borderline 'good' becomes more like 'good with caveat'. Use this when planning a sked or contest window.
Solar 10.7 cm radio flux in solar flux u: Solar 10.7 cm radio flux in solar flux units, identical to F10.7. Older amateur-radio literature uses ‘SFI’; modern ionospheric models use ‘F10.7’. The 27-day outlook reports daily predicted SFI from the ISES weekly product.
Solar radio flux at 2800 MHz, in solar f: Solar radio flux at 2800 MHz, in solar flux units (1 sfu = 10⁻²² W m⁻² Hz⁻¹). The canonical input to ionospheric models. Range ~65 (solar minimum) to 300+ (very active). Higher = more F-region ionization = better HF, especially above 14 MHz. Older literature calls this SFI.
Solar radio flux, EUV, and X-ray output. F10.7 is the canonical IRI ionospheric driver; X-ray flux drives D-region absorption; protons drive polar-cap absorption.
Solar wind {v} km/s. High-speed-stream a: Solar wind {v} km/s. High-speed-stream arrival; upper HF unsettled.
Solar wind {v} km/s. Strong high-speed-s: Solar wind {v} km/s. Strong high-speed-stream front; upper HF disturbed for 12 to 24 h.
Solar wind proton density at L1, in part: Solar wind proton density at L1, in particles/cm³. Quiet ~5; elevated values in slow streams and CME sheaths. Density and speed together set the dynamic pressure on the magnetosphere.
Solar wind ram pressure at L1, in nPa. P: Solar wind ram pressure at L1, in nPa. P_dyn = m_p × n × V² ≈ 1.6726×10⁻⁶ × n × V². Drives magnetopause compression: typical values 1-3 nPa, > 5 nPa moves the magnetopause inside ~7 R_E, > 10 nPa pushes it inside geosynchronous orbit. CME shock arrivals show as sudden P_dyn jumps.
Solar wind speed at L1, in km/s. Quiet ~: Solar wind speed at L1, in km/s. Quiet ~350; coronal-hole high-speed streams 500-800; CME shocks can exceed 1000 km/s.
Solar, space weather, and HF/VHF propaga: Solar, space weather, and HF/VHF propagation
Sporadic-E MUF divided by the band frequ: Sporadic-E MUF divided by the band frequency. ≥ 1 means an Es opening is supported on this band. Es openings are typically 50-144 MHz, brief (minutes to hours), and short-skip (~2000 km single hop).
Steep-angle skywave on the low bands (40: Steep-angle skywave on the low bands (40 m and below) for short paths (under 500 km). Signal goes nearly straight up, refracts off the F2 (or E) layer, and comes nearly straight down. Fills the skip zone where ground wave fades and oblique skywave overshoots.
Storm type (CME shock vs HSS stream): Storm type (CME shock vs HSS stream)
SWPC 3-day probabilities for radio black: SWPC 3-day probabilities for radio blackouts (R), radiation storms (S), and geomagnetic storms (G).
SWPC bulletin for a 10.7 cm (2800 MHz) s: SWPC bulletin for a 10.7 cm (2800 MHz) solar radio burst. Often pairs with M-class flares. Operationally a marker that the emitting region is energetically active; not directly an HF concern, but a useful concurrent flare indicator since the burst arrives within minutes of the flare peak.
SWPC bulletin for a planetary Kp alert o: SWPC bulletin for a planetary Kp alert or warning. Codes K04-K09 indicate Kp 4 (active) up to Kp 9 (extreme). Maps to the NOAA G-scale: Kp 5 = G1, Kp 9 = G5. Higher Kp means HF degradation widens equatorward and the auroral oval expands.
SWPC bulletin for a polar-cap absorption: SWPC bulletin for a polar-cap absorption event. Solar protons ionize the D-region inside the polar cap, producing severe HF absorption on any path that crosses above ~ 60 ° magnetic latitude. Can persist hours to days. Code prefix PC.
SWPC bulletin for a Type II or Type IV r: SWPC bulletin for a Type II or Type IV radio emission. A radio fingerprint of a coronal shock outpacing the solar wind (Type II almost always means a CME is on the way) or a long-duration energetic-electron source (Type IV). Operationally a heads-up to watch for proton events and CME arrival in 1 to 4 days.
SWPC bulletin for an integral solar prot: SWPC bulletin for an integral solar proton flux threshold crossing. PX1 = ≥ 1 MeV, PX10 = ≥ 10 MeV (this is the S-scale anchor: 10 pfu = S1), PX100 = ≥ 100 MeV. The ≥ 10 MeV channel drives polar-cap absorption, any HF path crossing above ~ 60 ° magnetic latitude is closed.
SWPC bulletin for an M-class solar X-ray: SWPC bulletin for an M-class solar X-ray flare. M1-M4.9 = NOAA R1; M5-M9.9 = R2. Causes minor-to-moderate HF blackouts on the sunlit hemisphere lasting tens of minutes; D-region absorption decays with the X-ray flux.
SWPC bulletin for an X-class solar X-ray: SWPC bulletin for an X-class solar X-ray flare. X1-X9.9 = NOAA R3; X10+ = R4-R5. Wide-area HF blackout on the sunlit side for ~1 h; the largest events can degrade HF for days and trigger proton events at Earth within hours.
SWPC bulletin for energetic-electron flu: SWPC bulletin for energetic-electron flux ≥ 2 MeV at GEO sustained above the EF3 threshold. An internal-charging risk for satellites in geostationary orbit; not directly an HF concern, but a useful sustained-disturbance indicator since electron events trail major storms by 1 to 3 days.
SWPC D-Region Absorption Prediction for: SWPC D-Region Absorption Prediction for this band frequency. Flagged here when modeled absorption ≥ 1 dB, i.e. the D-layer is eating signal before it can reach the reflective F-layer above. Caused by solar X-ray flares and proton events.
SWPC watch bulletin for an upcoming geom: SWPC watch bulletin for an upcoming geomagnetic storm, typically issued from a CME arrival forecast or an HSS interface prediction. Codes A50 / A70 correspond to G3 / G4 watches. Plan operations: HF on polar paths will fade and the auroral oval will expand.
The <strong>amateur radio community</str: The amateur radio community at large - the operators who tested early versions, reported when verdicts disagreed with what they were hearing on the air, and helped close the gap between the model and reality.
The band-table's Excellent tier means \": The band-table's Excellent tier means "the loudest path on this band has plenty of margin"; the DX badge says "that loud path is at least 6000 km long, so continent-crossing DX is open". Excellent without DX means the band is loud regionally (one or two short F2 hops) but the long-path reach isn't there. DX without the badge isn't impossible -- weaker paths can still work -- but the badge marks where the model expects DX to be easy.
The complete model is documented in the: The complete model is documented in the
The composite Kp value the propagation p: The composite Kp value the propagation physics actually consumes. Built from the instantaneous Kp plus three corrections: an exponentially-weighted lag kernel (the F-region keeps responding hours after Kp peaks), a Bz forward bump (sustained southward IMF at L1 leads the index by 30 to 60 min), and a Dst ring-current bump (intensification beyond what Kp alone captures). When this number exceeds the live Kp, the upper-HF verdicts are running ahead of the published index.
The continental sub-archive Wyoming grou: The continental sub-archive Wyoming groups this station under (europe, naconf for North America, samer for South America, africa, seasia for South / Southeast Asia and the southwest Pacific, ant for Antarctica). Carried for at-a-glance geography; ionocast hits the per-region URL for each station.
The full ionocast implementation - brows: The full ionocast implementation - browser code, Cloudflare Pages Functions for the upstream proxies, the offline calibration harness, and the LaTeX whitepaper - is open at github.com/topklc/ionocast.
The Ko-fi link in the footer is treated: The Ko-fi link in the footer is treated as non-commercial under each of the licenses above:
The lowest 1 km bends radio rays downwar: The lowest 1 km bends radio rays downward more than a standard atmosphere but not enough to trap them. Typical effects: the radio horizon extends past the geometric horizon, and tropospheric scatter paths run noticeably above their fair-weather budget. Common precursor to ducting; watch for the gradient steepening further.
The lowest 1 km has a refractivity gradi: The lowest 1 km has a refractivity gradient between roughly −79 and 0 N/km, the normal mid-latitude state. Radio rays bend gently toward Earth (the standard 4/3 effective Earth radius), but no duct or significant range extension. Default condition outside of marine inversions and warm-season stratification events.
The operators of every individual GIRO d: The operators of every individual GIRO digisonde station - the per-station provider list is fetched live alongside the data so each station is credited at the point of use, but the network as a whole is the foundation of the foF2 climatology.
The propagation mechanism that produces: The propagation mechanism that produces the highest margin on this band: F2 for ordinary skywave, NVIS for short low-band paths, Es for sporadic-E (single 2000 km hop off the E layer), Aurora for VHF auroral-E scatter, MS for meteor scatter. The model evaluates each mode in parallel and the winner drives the verdict.
The QTH (Maidenhead grid square) is stor: The QTH (Maidenhead grid square) is stored in the browser's localStorage and read only by the page's own JavaScript. The Ionosphere and VHF tropospheric panels send the QTH to ionocast's /api/giro and /api/tropo Cloudflare Pages Functions so each can find the nearest digisonde / radiosonde station. The proxy uses the QTH only to compute distances and never persists or forwards it to the upstream service. Cloudflare's edge logs may briefly capture the request URL per their standard privacy policy.
The site has no build step and no bundle: The site has no build step and no bundler. Everything served to the browser is plain HTML, ES modules, and CSS. The only third-party JavaScript is MapLibre GL JS + d3-contour + d3-array (used solely by the tropospheric ducting heatmap), all vendored locally. No CDN, no telemetry, no analytics, no tracking.
The tool runs entirely in the browser. L: The tool runs entirely in the browser. Live solar, geomagnetic, and ionospheric measurements are passed through an ITU-R P.533 link budget and mapped onto an ITU-R P.842 reliability bucket: excellent, good, fair, poor, or closed. The verdict reflects the operator's own station, not a generic reference receiver, and every refresh rederives the budget against the most recent observations.
Theme preference (light / dark / auto) a: Theme preference (light / dark / auto) and selected language live in localStorage.
This follows the standard Creative Commo: This follows the standard Creative Commons reading of the NC clause: the test is "primarily intended for or directed toward commercial advantage or monetary compensation," and a tip jar with no consideration in return does not meet it. See the CC NC FAQ for the canonical interpretation.
Top active regions by X-class flare prob: Top active regions by X-class flare probability from the SWPC daily report.
Total electrons in a vertical column thr: Total electrons in a vertical column through the ionosphere over the QTH, in TECU (10¹⁶ e⁻/m²). Higher = denser ionosphere = higher MUF. Sourced from the kc2g GNSS network.
Total interplanetary magnetic field magn: Total interplanetary magnetic field magnitude at L1, in nT. Combined with Bz, tells you how strong the field is and how much of it is southward (geoeffective).
Two streams: SWPC's official bulletins (: Two streams: SWPC's official bulletins (top 4 most recent, lagged hours behind real events) plus model-derived live alerts triggered by current conditions. Pill color carries severity (INFO gray, WATCH yellow, ALERT red, EXTREME deep red); click the pill for the tier definition. Body text describes the topic, underlined phrases (R-scale flares, G/S-scale events, Bz, Dst, D-RAP, HSS, forecast σ, storm main/recovery) are individually clickable for topic-specific detail.
Upstream queries to giro.uml.edu and wea: Upstream queries to giro.uml.edu and weather.uwyo.edu identify the nearest station by code only; the QTH-to-code translation happens on the ionocast proxy, so the upstream services never receive QTH coordinates.
Used for: 110m / 50m / 10m coastline, co: Used for: 110m / 50m / 10m coastline, country, and state-province outline vectors layered on top of the tropo heatmap. Three-tier LOD with cross-fade transitions between scales as the user zooms.
Used for: 13-level pressure profile (T,: Used for: 13-level pressure profile (T, RH, geopotential height, 1000-500 hPa) plus 2 m temperature, 2 m dewpoint, skin temperature, land-sea mask, surface pressure, orography, boundary-layer height, and 10 m winds from the GFS 0.25° forecast. Computed into the global tropospheric ducting heatmap (per-cell tropo_index per ITU-R P.453 and P.834).
Used for: 3-day forecast, 27-day outlook: Used for: 3-day forecast, 27-day outlook, solar regions, D-RAP grid, OVATION aurora, planetary Kp, F10.7 flux, GOES X-ray / protons / electrons, DSCOVR solar-wind mag + plasma, active alerts, animation frames.
Used for: ClickHouse API calls: per-band: Used for: ClickHouse API calls: per-band aggregate counts for the last hour, and parameterized historical spot counts for prediction scoring.
Used for: CME catalog (CMEAnalysis) and: Used for: CME catalog (CMEAnalysis) and coronal-hole high-speed stream catalog (HSS).
Used for: estimated International Sunspo: Used for: estimated International Sunspot Number (EISN).
Used for: HMI visible (sunspots), 193 Å: Used for: HMI visible (sunspots), 193 Å corona, 304 Å chromosphere latest frames, loaded directly as .
Used for: Hp30 / ap30 nowcast (and by ex: Used for: Hp30 / ap30 nowcast (and by extension the Kp index family).
Used for: marching-squares contour gener: Used for: marching-squares contour generation that converts the per-cell tropo_index grid into the filled-band polygons MapLibre paints. d3-array is a transitive dependency.
Used for: meteor-shower calendar (peak d: Used for: meteor-shower calendar (peak date, ZHR, window) for the 9 major showers, baked into src/derive/showers.js as a static lookup. Drives the "active showers" outlook panel and the VHF meteor-scatter verdict floor.
Used for: per-station digisonde characte: Used for: per-station digisonde characteristics (foF2, foE, foEs, hmF2, MUFD, MD) for the nearest ionosonde to the operator's QTH.
Used for: real-time ionosphere station J: Used for: real-time ionosphere station JSON (/api/stations.json) and the foF2 SVG render.
Used for: upper-air radiosonde soundings: Used for: upper-air radiosonde soundings (HTML parsed for temperature / dewpoint / pressure rows, converted to refractivity ΔN).
Used for: WebGL pan-zoom rendering of th: Used for: WebGL pan-zoom rendering of the tropo heatmap (filled-contour band layers + isolines) and the Natural Earth outline overlays. Vendored locally rather than served from a CDN; CSP connect-src stays at 'self'.
VHF signals scatter off the disturbed au: VHF signals scatter off the disturbed auroral E region during geomagnetic storms (Kp ≥ 5). Characteristic raspy / distorted CW note from Doppler smearing. Only viable on 6 m and 2 m at high latitudes; SSB barely intelligible.
Watch tier. An event is expected or a le: Watch tier. An event is expected or a leading indicator has crossed its threshold (substorm-precursor Bz, G1-onset Kp, S1 protons, M-class flare, Dst ≤ −50 nT, mid-strength HSS). Plan around the expected window; degradation is likely but not yet at full strength.
World Meteorological Organization 5-digi: World Meteorological Organization 5-digit station number plus a place name, identifying the radiosonde site whose sounding produced this row. The University of Wyoming archive is hit once per station; failed rows mean that site did not release a sounding for the current 00 Z or 12 Z slot.
WSPR queries to <code>db1.wspr.live</cod: WSPR queries to db1.wspr.live are global hourly aggregates with no grid field; the operator's location is never transmitted to the WSPR service.
Z-score of the current value vs its 27-d: Z-score of the current value vs its 27-day rolling baseline (one solar rotation): (x − mean) / stddev. Positive = unusually active for this point in the rotation; negative = unusually quiet. Strips out the slow Carrington-rotation trend so you can see today's anomaly.
